skill.name: perplexity-search
skill.description: Search current web and research sources with Perplexity models through LiteLLM and OpenRouter. The skill supports cited answers, model selection, setup checks, and optional result export.
skill.summary: Search current web information with Perplexity models through OpenRouter.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical combinations because this skill uses network access, API-key environment variables, file writes, and many shell examples in documentation. Manual review found no prompt injection, obfuscation, or unauthorized exfiltration; the outbound request is the advertised OpenRouter search path. Medium risk remains because the setup helper accepts and prints API keys, and both scripts can write to user-selected paths.

medium_findings.title: API Key Exposure Through Setup Helper
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The setup helper accepts OPENROUTER_API_KEY as a command-line argument, writes it to a .env-style file, and prints an export command containing the full key. This is not malicious, but command-line history, process listings, terminal logs, or copied output can expose the secret. Confidence: 0.90. Confidence reasoning: The exact API-key argument and full-key print behavior are visible in executable code, but the key is user-provided and used for the intended OpenRouter integration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The executable setup script directly handles and prints the user-provided API key. The behavior is intentional setup logic, so this is a credential hygiene risk rather than confirmed exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled File Write Paths
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The search script writes results to the path supplied by --output, and the setup script writes API-key configuration to the path supplied by --env-file. This can overwrite files writable by the user if the skill is invoked with unsafe paths. Confidence: 0.78. Confidence reasoning: The file writes are direct and executable, but they require explicit user-supplied paths and do not show privilege escalation or hidden persistence.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code opens user-selected paths for writing. The risk is bounded because there is no evidence of attacker-controlled path selection inside the skill.

low_findings.title: Expected Network Request to OpenRouter
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious exfiltration, TRUE_POSITIVE for external data transfer. The skill sends the user query to LiteLLM/OpenRouter to run Perplexity search, which is the documented function of the skill. Users should still treat submitted queries as data shared with an external API provider. Confidence: 0.88. Confidence reasoning: The network path is semantically aligned with the skill description and model setup, with no alternate suspicious endpoint found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code uses LiteLLM completion with the user query and the documented OpenRouter model prefix. No evidence found of hidden endpoints or unrelated data collection.

low_findings.title: Documentation Shell Examples Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for runtime command execution. Most external command findings are fenced shell examples in SKILL.md and reference guides, not commands run automatically by the skill. The batch example uses shell command substitution to name output files, so users should review paths before running it. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The flagged commands are in Markdown documentation, while the executable Python files do not call subprocess, os.system, or shell backticks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is inside Markdown examples and not executed by the skill itself. One example still deserves user caution because it demonstrates shell substitution.

low_findings.title: Environment File Guidance is Legitimate but Sensitive
low_findings.description: Verdict: NEEDS_REVIEW as operational guidance. The reference guide instructs users to store keys in shell profiles or .env files and includes .gitignore patterns for secrets. This is common setup documentation, but secrets stored in plaintext files require careful local handling. Confidence: 0.74. Confidence reasoning: The guidance includes security warnings and ignore patterns, but it still encourages plaintext secret storage that can be mishandled by users.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly documents plaintext environment storage. The same document also warns users not to commit keys, reducing but not eliminating risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Plaintext Secret Handling in CLI Setup
dangerous_patterns.description: The setup helper places a user-provided API key into a plaintext environment file and prints a direct export command. This pattern should be replaced or documented with safer input handling before broad distribution. Confidence: 0.90. Confidence reasoning: The relevant lines directly write and print the API key; no inference is required.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The setup flow directly handles the secret in plaintext and exposes it in terminal output.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unrestricted Output Path Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The CLI writes JSON results to any path supplied through --output and writes configuration to any path supplied through --env-file. This is a bounded local-file risk for user-invoked commands. Confidence: 0.78. Confidence reasoning: The direct open calls confirm the write pattern, but no malicious default path or stealth behavior was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The writes are present and depend on user-controlled CLI arguments.

content.value_statement: Current web and research questions often need information beyond a model cutoff. This skill uses Perplexity models through OpenRouter to return cited, current answers.
actual_capabilities: Runs Perplexity searches through LiteLLM and OpenRouter.
Supports Sonar, Sonar Pro, Sonar Pro Search, and reasoning models.
Checks whether LiteLLM and OPENROUTER_API_KEY are configured.
Prints cited answers and token usage details in verbose mode.
Can save search results to a user-selected JSON file.
Includes reference guides for query design, model choice, and setup.
limitations: Requires a valid OpenRouter API key and account credits.
Sends query text to an external API provider.
Citation availability depends on the model response returned by LiteLLM.
Setup helper stores keys in plaintext environment files when used.

use_cases.title: Find recent literature
use_cases.description: Search for current papers, preprints, and technical updates with citations for follow-up review.

use_cases.title: Verify current technical facts
use_cases.description: Check recent documentation, model availability, and ecosystem changes before making implementation decisions.

use_cases.title: Compare emerging options
use_cases.description: Use model selection guidance to compare technologies, products, or methods using current public sources.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Current Search
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for current information about {topic}. Include sources and note publication dates when available.

prompt_templates.title: Recent Literature Scan
prompt_templates.prompt: Find recent studies about {research_question} from {date_range}. Prioritize peer-reviewed sources and summarize key findings.

prompt_templates.title: Technical Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare {option_a} and {option_b} for {use_case}. Include current limitations, adoption signals, and cited evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Evidence Synthesis
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the most capable Perplexity model to synthesize current evidence on {complex_topic}. Separate consensus, uncertainty, and source quality.

output_examples.input: Search for recent CRISPR base editing clinical updates.
output_examples.output: A concise answer summarizing recent developments, named studies, reported outcomes, and source citations for verification.

output_examples.input: Compare Sonar Pro Search and Sonar Reasoning Pro for a research workflow.
output_examples.output: A practical comparison of model fit, cost tradeoffs, reasoning depth, and when each model should be selected.

output_examples.input: Verify the latest release status for a machine learning framework.
output_examples.output: A current status summary with dates, version notes, official-source links, and caveats about source freshness.

best_practices: Use specific questions with dates, domain terms, and desired evidence types.
Set spending limits in OpenRouter before using expensive search models.
Review citations directly before relying on medical, legal, or financial claims.
anti_patterns: Do not send private, regulated, or confidential data unless your OpenRouter use allows it.
Do not paste API keys into shared chats, issue trackers, or committed files.
Do not use broad prompts when a focused query would produce safer results.

faq.question: Does this skill require a Perplexity account?
faq.answer: No. It uses Perplexity models through OpenRouter with an OpenRouter API key.

faq.question: Does it browse the web directly from Claude, Codex, or Claude Code?
faq.answer: No. It calls Perplexity models through LiteLLM and OpenRouter from the included Python script.

faq.question: Can it save results for later review?
faq.answer: Yes. The search script can write results to a user-selected output file.

faq.question: What information leaves my machine?
faq.answer: Your query and API request data are sent to the external model provider through OpenRouter.

faq.question: Which model should I start with?
faq.answer: Start with Sonar Pro for balanced quality and cost, then upgrade for complex multi-step research.

faq.question: Is this safe for secrets or private research data?
faq.answer: Use caution. The setup helper handles API keys, and query text is sent to an external provider.
